resources.string: • A transformation moves every point to form a new figure. \n • Central symmetry reflects points across a center. \n • Axial symmetry reflects across a line; homothety scales from a center.
• Circles and parallelograms have centers of symmetry. \n • Shapes like triangles and squares have specific symmetry axes. \n • Homothetic figures share the same shape and center.
• Find point M on line p minimizing AM+MB. \n • Reflect B across p to get B′ and join A to B′. \n • Intersection with p gives the required M.
• A motion keeps distances unchanged (isometry). \n • Translation shifts points equally in one direction. \n • Rotation turns a figure around a fixed point.
• Motions preserve shapes, lengths, and angles. \n • Combining motions yields another motion. \n • Every figure is congruent to itself and others by motion.
• Symmetries are motions since they preserve distance. \n • Each translation between points is unique. \n • Proves key isometric properties.
• A similitude scales distances by a constant ratio k. \n • Figures are similar if one maps to the other by such a transformation. \n • Homothety is a special case of similitude.
• Similitude keeps lines straight and angles equal. \n • Preserves order of points along a line. \n • Homothety is always a type of similitude.
• Homothety with ratio k multiplies all distances by k. \n • Proven using similar triangles MON and M′ON′. \n • Confirms it’s a transformation of similitude.
